meta.name: Aortic Dissection — Tearing Chest/Back Pain with BP Differential, Pain Control, Gentle Transport

patient.name: Robert Mahoney
patient.chiefComplaint: Sudden, severe tearing chest pain that started about 35 minutes ago while loading firewood, radiating from his anterior chest through to between his shoulder blades; rated 10/10 and described as 'ripping.'

history.hpi: 65-year-old male was loading split firewood from the bed of his pickup truck onto a hand cart when, mid-lift, he was struck with a sudden, severe tearing pain in his anterior chest that immediately radiated through to his upper back between the shoulder blades. He describes it as 'like something ripped inside me' and rates it 10/10. He fell to one knee against the truck and could not move further. He felt diaphoretic and lightheaded but did not lose consciousness. His wife was on the porch and ran out, then called 911. He has had hypertension for at least 20 years that he 'mostly takes medicine for' but admits he runs out occasionally and has not had a clinic visit in 14 months. Long-time smoker (40+ pack-years, currently 1 ppd). His father died at age 62 of 'something with his heart, they couldn't fix it' — no other family details. Denies recent illness, denies prior similar pain, denies trauma other than the lifting effort. No use of cocaine or stimulants.
pastMedical: Active tobacco use — 40+ pack-years, currently 1 pack/day
Hyperlipidemia (untreated; last cholesterol panel 3 years ago)
No prior MI, no prior stroke, no known aneurysm
Family history of premature cardiac death in father at age 62
medications: Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily (admits to inconsistent adherence; runs out for days at a time)
Ibuprofen 200 mg PO PRN for knee pain
No statin, no antiplatelet, no anticoagulant
history.lastOralIntake: Coffee and a sandwich about 90 minutes ago
history.events: Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to back during firewood lift ~35 minutes ago; immediate 10/10 pain, fell to one knee, became diaphoretic and lightheaded. No syncope, no trauma other than the lift. Wife called 911 from the porch.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 65-year-old male, complaint of severe chest and back pain after lifting at home. Conscious, on the ground next to a pickup truck. Wife on scene. ALS response.
scene.location: Driveway of a rural single-family home. Patient kneeling/leaning against the side of his pickup truck near a wood pile. Concrete driveway, mild slope toward the road. Stretcher access requires crossing 8 feet of uneven driveway.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Wood pile and chainsaw secured by adult son who arrived. Pickup truck engine off. Wife cooperative.
scene.bystanders: Wife (primary historian) confirms timeline: husband was loading firewood, suddenly cried out, dropped to one knee, said 'something ripped inside me.' She called 911 within a minute. Adult son just arrived from next door. They confirm he has hypertension and is inconsistent with his pills.
visualCues: Heavyset man on one knee leaning against the side of a pickup truck, both hands gripping his anterior chest
Pale and visibly diaphoretic; gritting his teeth in obvious severe pain
Loose firewood scattered on the driveway behind him
Wife crouched beside him with a hand on his shoulder, holding his hat
Pill bottles brought out by son: lisinopril, HCTZ — visible refill dates lapsed on lisinopril
No external trauma visible; no head strike
Right hand grip is full strength when he reaches; left hand grip noticeably weaker when he tries

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds a 65-year-old man with classic features of acute aortic dissection: SUDDEN tearing pain radiating from anterior chest to interscapular back, severe HTN with longstanding poor control, heavy smoking history, and family history of premature cardiac death. Critical EMS exam findings: a measurable BP DIFFERENTIAL between arms (right arm 198/110, left arm 152/90 — a >40 mmHg gap), and a subtle left-arm grip weakness with intact right grip. 12-lead shows sinus rhythm at 92 with NO ST elevation and only nonspecific changes — this is NOT STEMI. The protocol pathway here is recognition of the pattern, OPIOID pain control (fentanyl preferred), CAUTIOUS BP reduction toward target SBP 100–120 to lower wall shear stress, GENTLE transport with minimal jostling, and routing to a CARDIOTHORACIC/VASCULAR-SURGICAL center — not the closest hospital. Aspirin is deferred or held pending physician guidance because this is not ACS and antiplatelet therapy can worsen bleeding if the dissection ruptures into the pericardium.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 24, splinting from pain, lungs clear bilaterally, no rales/wheeze
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Bilateral radial pulses present but RIGHT clearly stronger than LEFT; carotids equal and strong; cap refill 2 sec; cool diaphoretic skin
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Right grip strong; LEFT grip noticeably weaker (subtle); no facial droop; sensation intact bilaterally
otherFindings: BP RIGHT arm 198/110; BP LEFT arm 152/90 — >40 mmHg differential = key dissection sign
Tearing pain anterior chest → interscapular back (10/10)
Risk factors: longstanding poorly controlled HTN, 40+ pack-years smoking, family history
Subtle left-arm weakness — possible left subclavian/carotid involvement
12-lead: NSR 92, NO ST elevation, nonspecific T-wave flattening only
Lungs clear, no JVD, no rales — distinguishes from CHF/PE
Pathway: opioid analgesia, cautious BP reduction, GENTLE transport, vascular surgery center
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (through clenched teeth) Something ripped. Right here, then through to my back. Worst pain… ever. Help me.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize tearing-pain + back radiation + risk factors → high suspicion aortic dissection
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Inclusion + Educational Pearls — chest pain pattern recognition; vague/atypical complaints common in elderly; pattern of tearing radiation to back is not ACS-typical and warrants dissection workup.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Acute coronary syndrome may present with atypical pain, vague or only generalized complaints. Ischemic burden time is a risk for morbidity and mortality, EMS can help decrease first medical contact to intervention time/reflow by efficient scripting/training of safely minimizing scene time.

expectedActions.shortAction: BP BOTH arms; check pulse symmetry; document a >20 mmHg differential as key finding
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Assessment Exam 2b + ACS Pertinent Findings — circulation exam includes pulses bilaterally; bilateral BP is the discriminator that catches dissection before EKG/STEMI activation.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Exam: Airway/breathing (airway edema, rales, wheezing, pulse oximetry, respiratory rate). Circulation (heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill). Abdomen (hepatomegaly). Mucous membrane hydration. Skin (turgor, rash). Neurologic (GCS, sensorimotor deficits).

expectedActions.shortAction: 12-lead EKG; look for STEMI; here: NO ST elevation — do NOT activate STEMI alert
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 4 — 12-lead within 10 minutes; if no STEMI on EKG, the diagnosis is NOT acute coronary occlusion; do not anchor on 'chest pain = MI'.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The 12-lead EKG is the primary diagnostic tool that identifies a STEMI; it is imperative that EMS clinicians routinely acquire a 12-lead EKG within 10 minutes for all patients exhibiting signs and symptoms of ACS. Performance of serial EKGs is encouraged for symptomatic patients with EKGs initially non-diagnostic for STEMI.

expectedActions.shortAction: IV access ×2; cardiac monitor; SpO2; defib pads on (high-risk patient)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Patient Safety #3 — defibrillator pads on high-risk patients; large-bore IVs needed for analgesia and possible volume support.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Observe for signs of clinical deterioration: dysrhythmias, chest pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness/syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension. Consider placing defibrillator pads on high-risk patients.

expectedActions.shortAction: Fentanyl 95 mcg IV (95 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 95 mcg, max 100 mcg) — analgesia FIRST
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 6b + ACS step 9 — fentanyl 1 mcg/kg max 100 mcg for moderate-to-severe pain; opioids are the field analgesic when nitrates are inadequate or contraindicated.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Pain Management
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For Moderate to Severe pain, analgesics include: Fentanyl: 1 mcg/kg IN, IM, IV or IO (maximum initial dose of 100 mcg). If indicated based on pain assessment, and vital signs allow, repeat pain medication administration (excluding acetaminophen and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines) after 5 minutes of the previous dose.

expectedActions.shortAction: Defer/hold aspirin pending dissection workup; consult medical control or receiving MD
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 3 — defer aspirin and nitrates when likely non-cardiac chest pain. Tearing pain with BP differential and no STEMI on EKG fits a non-ACS pattern; antiplatelet therapy can worsen bleeding if dissection ruptures.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Some patients will present with likely non-cardiac chest pain and otherwise have a low likelihood of ACS (e.g., blunt trauma to the chest of a child). For these patients, defer the administration of aspirin (ASA) and nitrates per the Pain Management Guideline.

transitions.if: Fentanyl given; cautious BP control; gentle transport prep
transitions.condition: Crew gives fentanyl 95 mcg IV for pain, holds aspirin pending physician consult, and prepares gentle transport to a cardiothoracic-vascular surgical center; pain decreases, BP starts to come down with analgesia alone (sympathetic tone reduction); vitals reassessed.

transitions.if: Treated as STEMI/ACS; bilateral BP not done
transitions.condition: Crew anchored on 'chest pain = ACS' without checking bilateral BPs, gave aspirin 324 mg PO, and transported to the closest community hospital instead of a cardiothoracic-surgical center; en route the dissection extends, causing further hemodynamic instability and a delay in definitive surgical care.

phases.name: Fentanyl given; gentle transport prep
phases.description: Crew gives fentanyl 95 mcg IV (calc: 95 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 95 mcg, capped at 100 mcg max). Pain reduces from 10/10 to 6/10 within 5 minutes and BP softens with sympathetic tone reduction (right arm 168/96, left arm 138/82 — differential narrows but persists). Aspirin held; medical control consulted and concurs with deferring antiplatelet therapy and routing to the regional cardiothoracic surgery center despite the longer transport (~25 minutes) versus the closer community ED (~10 minutes). Crew lifts and moves the patient with minimal jostling, maintaining a head-up position of comfort, and transports gently — every bump and accel/decel adds shear stress to a tearing aorta.
phases.triggerCondition: Fentanyl 95 mcg IV given; aspirin held with medical control concurrence; pain and BP softening with analgesia; gentle transport initiated to cardiothoracic-vascular surgical center.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Right radial > left radial still detectable but less dramatic; cap refill 2 sec; warming
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Subtle left-arm weakness persists; right grip strong
otherFindings: Pain 6/10 (down from 10/10) on fentanyl 95 mcg IV
BP RIGHT 168/96, BP LEFT 138/82 — differential still present
Aspirin held; medical control concurs
Routing to regional CT/vascular surgery center (~25 min) — confirmed accepting
Patient packaged with HOB up 30°, padding around stretcher edges, securing devices to minimize jostling
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: The medicine helped a lot, thank you. Still hurts but I can talk. Just please don't bump me.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess pain (10/10 → 6/10); repeat fentanyl q5 min PRN if VS allow (max as protocol)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 9 + Documentation — repeat dose after 5 min if needed and vitals allow; document initial and reassessed pain scores.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Pain Management
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If indicated based on pain assessment, and vital signs allow, repeat pain medication administration (excluding acetaminophen and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines) after 5 minutes of the previous dose. Documentation of patient reassessment with repeat vital signs and patient pain scale assessment.

expectedActions.shortAction: Consider NTG 0.4 mg SL ONCE for SBP control (target 100–120) — only if pain inadequately controlled and SBP >100
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 7 — NTG 0.4 mg SL if SBP >100, with caution and readiness to respond to hypotension; in dissection, the goal is to reduce wall shear stress, but BP must not be overshot below perfusion threshold.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (SL), can repeat q (quaque, every) 3–5 minutes if SBP greater than 100 mmHg. Care should always be taken when giving nitroglycerin when the patient's blood pressure is marginal. If used in this setting, the clinician should weigh the risk and benefit of nitrate administration over the administration of an opiate analgesic and be ready to respond to hypotension with fluid bolus or pressor.

expectedActions.shortAction: Consider metoprolol 5 mg IV slow (SBP >120) for HR/dP-dt reduction — if available and per medical control
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Adult Management — metoprolol 5 mg IV q5 min ×3 doses; in dissection, beta-blockade reduces dP/dt (rate of pressure rise) which is the key shear-stress driver. Use only with adequate SBP and per medical control given the patient is not formally tachycardic.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Tachycardia with a Pulse
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Metoprolol 5 mg IV given over 1–2 minutes. May repeat as needed q 5 minutes for a total of 3 doses. Administer metoprolol to patients with SBP greater than 120 mmHg. Worsening CHF, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), asthma, as well as hypotension and bradycardia can occur with use of metoprolol.

expectedActions.shortAction: HOB 30°; padded stretcher; minimize jostling — wall shear is the killer
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 4 + 10 — non-pharmacologic measures, position of comfort, transport in position of comfort and reassess as indicated.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Pain Management
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Non-pharmacologic pain management options include: Placement of the patient in a position of comfort. Application of ice packs and/or splints for pain secondary to trauma. Verbal reassurance to control anxiety. Transport in position of comfort and reassess as indicated.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to cardiothoracic/vascular surgery center — bypass closest if necessary
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 10 — transport and destination decisions based on local resources and system of care; dissection requires surgical capability, not just stenting.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Transport and destination decisions should be based on local resources and system of care.

expectedActions.shortAction: Advance notice: 'Suspected aortic dissection — BP differential, tearing pain, no STEMI'
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 11 — early notification to receiving facility of any changes; for suspected dissection, advance notice mobilizes CT, blood bank, OR, and surgical team.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Early notification to receiving facility of any changes in patient condition or serial EKGs. Advance notification should be provided to the receiving hospital for patients identified as having a STEMI.

transitions.if: Stable on analgesia; gentle transport to vascular center
transitions.condition: Pain controlled, BP softening with analgesia, dissection not propagating clinically; gentle transport to cardiothoracic surgical center; serial reassessment for pulse-deficit progression, mentation change, syncope, or new murmur (aortic insufficiency from proximal extension).

phases.name: Stable on analgesia; gentle transport to vascular center
phases.description: Patient transported gently to regional cardiothoracic/vascular surgical center. Driver coached to drive smoothly — no hard accel/decel, no rough lane changes, cushion every bump. Pain reassessed every 5 minutes; fentanyl repeated once at half-dose for breakthrough pain (50 mcg given) per protocol allowance with VS adequate. Continuous monitoring includes bilateral BPs every 5 minutes, pulse symmetry checks, neurologic status, and serial 12-leads. Receiving facility has prepped CT angiography on the gantry and the cardiothoracic surgery team is mobilized.
phases.triggerCondition: Pain controlled with analgesia; dissection clinically stable on transport; gentle conveyance to cardiothoracic surgical center with serial reassessment of bilateral BPs, pulse symmetry, mentation, and rhythm.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Pulses present bilaterally with right slightly stronger than left; warm and dry
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Subtle left-arm weakness unchanged; sensation intact bilaterally
otherFindings: BP RIGHT 154/88, BP LEFT 130/76 — differential persistent but stable
Pain 4/10 after second small fentanyl bolus
No new neuro deficits, no new pulse changes, no new murmur
Receiving CT-surgical team mobilized; CT angio bay ready
Wife following separately
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I'm scared but I can breathe okay. Tell my wife I love her if anything happens. Thanks for being calm.

expectedActions.shortAction: Bilateral BPs q5 min; document pulse symmetry; flag widening differential
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Patient Safety #1 + Shock Documentation — observe for clinical deterioration; full vitals q15 min or more frequently as appropriate; bilateral BPs are the dissection-specific surveillance.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Full vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment) with reassessment q 15 minutes or more frequently as appropriate.

expectedActions.shortAction: Serial 12-leads — watch for new ST changes (proximal extension into coronaries)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Patient Safety #2 — perform serial 12-lead EKGs especially if clinical changes are noted; dissection extension into RCA can produce inferior STEMI later.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Perform serial 12-lead EKGs (especially if clinical changes are noted).

expectedActions.shortAction: Serial neuro checks: grip, facial droop, speech, mentation — extension marker
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Assessment Exam 2f — neurologic assessment (GCS, sensorimotor deficits); proximal dissection extension can cause stroke from carotid involvement.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Exam: Neurologic (GCS, sensorimotor deficits).

expectedActions.shortAction: Bedside handoff with bilateral BP trend, fentanyl timeline, neuro exam, EKG strips
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Documentation — time of symptom onset, contact-to-EKG, ASA decision (held with reason), STEMI notification status, time of arrival.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: The time of symptom onset. The time of patient contact by EMS to the time of 12-lead EKG acquisition. The time aspirin (ASA) administered, or reason why not given. The time of STEMI notification.

phases.name: Treated as STEMI/ACS; bilateral BP missed
phases.description: Crew anchored on 'chest pain = MI' without performing bilateral BPs and pattern-matched to ACS: aspirin 324 mg PO chewed, NTG 0.4 mg SL, and routing to the closest community ED (no cardiothoracic surgical capability). Within 10 minutes en route, the dissection extends — pain abruptly recurs, the right arm BP shoots up while the left becomes weak, the patient develops a new diastolic murmur (proximal extension causing aortic insufficiency), and mentation begins to cloud as cerebral perfusion suffers. The community ED, on receiving the patient, has to recognize the dissection on CT and arrange a critical-care interfacility transfer to a surgical center — a 60–90 minute delay in definitive therapy. The teaching points are: (1) bilateral BPs are the EMS-level discriminator that prevents this anchoring error; (2) destination decisions matter; (3) aspirin and antiplatelets in an undifferentiated severe-pain patient with risk factors for dissection can worsen bleeding if rupture occurs.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew failed to check bilateral BPs, pattern-matched chest pain to ACS, gave aspirin and nitro, and routed to a non-surgical hospital; en route the dissection extended with new murmur, clouded mentation, and propagating BP differential.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: RIGHT radial bounding; LEFT radial weak/thready; cap refill 3 sec on left; new diastolic murmur at right upper sternal border
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: LEFT arm now grossly weaker; subtle right facial droop emerging
otherFindings: Aspirin 324 mg PO already chewed and swallowed
NTG 0.4 mg SL given — softened BP overall but did not address dP/dt
Pain recurred to 9/10 then 'changed character' to abdominal/flank as dissection extended distally
BP RIGHT 196/108, BP LEFT now 92/56 — differential WIDENING (>100 mmHg)
New diastolic murmur — proximal extension with aortic insufficiency
Routing problem: closest hospital is non-surgical; need to redirect or arrange interfacility ASAP
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (slow, clouded) The pain came back. It moved. Down here now. I can't… feel my left arm right. What's happening?

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize anchoring error: bilateral BPs now confirm dissection — redirect care plan
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Patient Safety #1 — observe for clinical deterioration; reassess differential when new findings emerge; bilateral BPs are the late-but-critical discriminator.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Observe for signs of clinical deterioration: dysrhythmias, chest pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness/syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension. Perform serial 12-lead EKGs (especially if clinical changes are noted).

expectedActions.shortAction: Fentanyl 95 mcg IV NOW (95 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) for renewed 9/10 pain
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 6b — fentanyl 1 mcg/kg max 100 mcg; severe pain analgesia is independent of the diagnostic uncertainty.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Pain Management
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For Moderate to Severe pain, analgesics include: Fentanyl: 1 mcg/kg IN, IM, IV or IO (maximum initial dose of 100 mcg).

expectedActions.shortAction: Notify receiving ED of dissection findings; request immediate transfer arrangement on arrival
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 11 + step 10 — early notification of changes; destination decisions are continuous, not one-time; mobilize the next step of care preemptively.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Transport and destination decisions should be based on local resources and system of care. Early notification to receiving facility of any changes in patient condition or serial EKGs.

expectedActions.shortAction: Medical control consult: BP control with metoprolol IV (SBP >120) for dP/dt reduction
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Adult Management — metoprolol 5 mg IV q5 min ×3; in dissection, beta-blockade reduces shear stress; coordinate with medical control given the off-label rationale.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Tachycardia with a Pulse
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Metoprolol 5 mg IV given over 1–2 minutes. May repeat as needed q 5 minutes for a total of 3 doses. Administer metoprolol to patients with SBP greater than 120 mmHg.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document timeline; debrief: bilateral BPs and tearing-pain pattern as dissection-vs-MI rule
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Documentation — time of symptom onset, ASA timing, STEMI notification; the audit trail surfaces the anchoring point so it can be trained against next time.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: The time of symptom onset. The time of patient contact by EMS to the time of 12-lead EKG acquisition. The time aspirin (ASA) administered, or reason why not given. The time of STEMI notification.
